'''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water.
Misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the Ki Society Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise.

'''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water.
Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise.
Before under going misogi people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a lion cloth and head band. they then begin to shakethe soul by bouncing their hand in from of the stomache(hara in "tai chi chuan")to become aware of the soul's presence within. they the star warm up calisthenics called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocationsthat activate the soul, afirm the potentialfor realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the Kami.
Before enterin the waterfall the participants raise their metabolism and absorbing as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given sake to sprat into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader coutns to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while contiuously chanting the word "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!"this phrase asks the Kami to wash away the tsumi from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. {{reli-stub}} [[Category:Shinto]]
[[de:Misogi]]

The participants then enter the waterfall while contiuously chanting the word "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!"this phrase asks the Kami to wash away the tsumi from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
citation: Fisher, mary pat . living religions. 5 th ed. : prentice hall, . {{reli-stub}} [[Category:Shinto]]

revision.comment: I inserted the appropriate spaces between words, cleaned up the spelling, and corrected some punctuatuion.
revision.text:
'''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water.
Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise.
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in from of the stomach (hara in "tai chi chuan")to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up calisthenics called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that activate the soul, affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the Kami.
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their metabolism and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given sake to spit into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader coutns to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while contiuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the tsumi from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
citation: Fisher, Mary Pat . Living Religions. 5 th ed. : prentice hall, . {{reli-stub}} [[Category:Shinto]]
[[de:Misogi]]

'''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise.
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in from of the stomach (hara in "[[tai chi chuan]]") to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up [[calisthenics]] called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that activate the soul, affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the Kami.
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while contiuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the tsumi from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5 th ed. : Prentice Hall.
{{reli-stub}} [[Category:Shinto]]
[[de:Misogi]]

==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5 th ed. : Prentice Hall.

'''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. At [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]], misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings.
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in from of the stomach to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up [[calisthenics]] called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that activate the soul, affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the Kami.
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while contiuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the tsumi from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5 th ed. : Prentice Hall.
{{reli-stub}} [[Category:Shinto]]
[[de:Misogi]]

Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that activate the soul, affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the Kami.
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. '''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. At [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]], misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings.
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in from of the stomach to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up [[calisthenics]] called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that activate the soul, affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the Kami.
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while contiuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the tsumi from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "centre". The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to compliment his training and search for perfection ==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5 th ed. : Prentice Hall.
{{reli-stub}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bathing]]
[[de:Misogi]]

Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "centre". The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to compliment his training and search for perfection ==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5 th ed. : Prentice Hall.

[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|right|thumb|300px|Night misogi under cold waterfall at Tsubaki Great Shrine, Japan]] '''Misogi''' is a [[Shinto]] practice involving [[ritual purification|purification]] in a waterfall or other natural running water. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. At [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]], misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings.
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in front of the stomach to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up [[calisthenics]] called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that are said to activate the [[soul]], affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the [[Kami]].
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the water fall in three mouthfuls the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this Impurity. revision.comment: Edited first paragraph to clarify the fact that there are other kinds of misogi besides "water" misogi.
revision.text:
[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|right|thumb|300px|Night misogi under cold waterfall at Tsubaki Great Shrine, Japan]] {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊|''Misogi''}} is a [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification|purification]]. This purification may be undertaken through exhaustive activities such as extended periods without sleep, breath training [http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-PHIL/ew88808.htm], standing under waterfalls, or other methods. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices. The bulk of this article will deal with "water" misogi.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. At [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]], misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's famous [[Otowa no taki]], or Sound-of-Wings Waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them ([http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm 1]).
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in front of the stomach to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up [[calisthenics]] called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that are said to activate the [[soul]], affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the [[Kami]].
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. The leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the [[tsumi]] from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "[[hara (Martial Arts)|hara]]", or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.
==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5th ed. : Prentice Hall.
==See also== *[[Preliminary Misogi Rite]]
{{reli-stub}}

Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on white kimono and headband. Men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin to "shake the soul" by bouncing their hand in front of the stomach to become aware of the soul's presence within. They then start warm-up [[calisthenics]] called bird rowing. Following the leader they begin to shout invocations that are said to activate the [[soul]], affirm the potential for realizing one's own soul, and unifying the people with the [[Kami]].
Before entering the waterfall the participants raise their [[metabolism]] and absorb as much ki as possible by a special form of deep breathing. They are sprinkled with purifying salt and are given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. The leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the [[tsumi]] from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "[[hara (Martial Arts)|hara]]", or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.
==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5th ed. : Prentice Hall.
==See also== *[[Preliminary Misogi Rite]]

revision.comment: Minor edit....kanji and a more general explanation.
revision.text:
[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|right|thumb|300px|Night misogi under cold waterfall at Tsubaki Great Shrine, Japan]] {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊|''Misogi''}} is a [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification|purification]]. This purification may be undertaken through exhaustive activities such as extended periods without sleep, breath training [http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-PHIL/ew88808.htm], standing under waterfalls, or other methods. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices. The bulk of this article will deal with "water" misogi.
There is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. At [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]], misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's famous [[Otowa no taki]], or Sound-of-Wings Waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them ([http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm 1]).
Before undergoing misogi, people undergo preliminary purification practices. Women put on a white kimono and a headband. Men put on a loin cloth and a head band. They then begin 降り魂 'furitama' or "soul shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them from down to up, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of / unified with the soul's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] called 鳥船'tori-fune" or "bird (boat) rowing". These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by words or incantations. After, the "leader" begins to speak out invocations that are said to activate the [[soul]]. The "followes" generally speak with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own soul, and thus unifying them with the [[Kami]] around them.
The above excercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls; Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the [[sin]] from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "[[hara (Martial Arts)|hara]]", or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.
==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5th ed. : Prentice Hall.
==See also== *[[Preliminary Misogi Rite]]

revision.comment: Minor edit...Spirit and Soul are almost identical in English...however the term "spirit" is a more common/relative terms in Japanese.
revision.text:
[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|right|thumb|300px|Night misogi under cold waterfall at Tsubaki Great Shrine, Japan]] {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊|''Misogi''}} is a [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification|purification]]. This purification may be undertaken through exhaustive activities such as extended periods without sleep, breath training [http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-PHIL/ew88808.htm], standing under waterfalls, or other methods. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices. The bulk of this article will deal with "water" misogi.
There are several different groups practicing Misogi in Japan, perhaps one of the most well known is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. Another well known group is under the [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]]. Misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's famous [[Otowa no taki]], or Sound-of-Wings Waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them ([http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm 1]). Every year, many groups take pilgrimages to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan.
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, Women put on a special white kimono and a headband and Men put aloin cloth and head band. They then begin 降り魂 '''furitama''' or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of / unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] called 鳥船 '''tori-fune''' or "bird (boat) rowing". These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayer words or incantations. After, the "leader" begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The "followers" generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[Kami]] around them.
The above excercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls; Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the [[sin]] from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "[[hara (Martial Arts)|hara]]", or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.

Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, Women put on a special white kimono and a headband and Men put aloin cloth and head band. They then begin 降り魂 '''furitama''' or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of / unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] called 鳥船 '''tori-fune''' or "bird (boat) rowing". These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayer words or incantations. After, the "leader" begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The "followers" generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[Kami]] around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls; Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.

The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. Paul Healey a third dan from manchester U.K. His practice sessions were legendary for their misogi content,Aikido is, "more than just tecnique," was his mantra. He is now a soundsculpting film maker living in St Ives Cornwall U.K where he ran a very successful aikido club for a number of years.He is still an advocate of misogi and can seen daily testing himself against the rigours of the Atlantic ocean.

{{Shintoism}}
[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|right|thumb|300px|Night misogi under cold waterfall at Tsubaki Great Shrine, Japan]] {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊}} is a Japanese [[yamabushi|mountain ascetic]] practice of [[ritual purification|lustration]]. This may be undertaken through exhaustive activities such as extended periods without sleep, breath training [http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-PHIL/ew88808.htm], standing under waterfalls, or other methods. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices. The bulk of this article will deal with "water" misogi.
There are several different groups practicing Misogi in Japan, perhaps one of the most well known is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. Another well known group is under the [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]]. Misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's famous [[Otowa no taki]], or Sound-of-Wings Waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them ([http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm 1]). Every year, many groups take pilgrimages to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan.
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin 降り魂 '''furitama''' or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of / unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] called 鳥船 '''tori-fune''' or "bird (boat) rowing". These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayer words or incantations. After, the "leader" begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The "followers" generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[Kami]] around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls; Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "yei!" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!" This phrase asks the Kami to wash away the [[sin]] from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your "[[Dantian|hara]]", or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.
==Citations== #Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5th ed. : Prentice Hall.
==See also== *[[Preliminary Misogi Rite]]

Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a loin cloth and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the ''[[kami]]'' around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "''harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!''" This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the [[sin]] from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your ''[[Dantian|one-point]]'', or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.

{{onesource|date=February 2008}} [[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|right|thumb|300px|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[Tsubaki]] Shrine]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊}} is a Japanese [[yamabushi|mountain ascetic]] practice of [[ritual purification]]. This may be undertaken through exhaustive activities such as extended periods without sleep, breath training,<ref>[http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-PHIL/ew88808.htm Mind and Morality in Nineteenth-Century Japanese Religions: Misogi-Kyo and Maruyama-Kyo] By Janine Anderson Sawada Philosophy East & West V. 48 No. 1 (January 1998) pp. 108-141 Copyright 1998 by [[University of Hawaii]] Press</ref> standing under [[waterfall]]s, or other methods. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.
There are several different groups practicing misogi in Japan, perhaps one of the best known is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. Another well known group is under the [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]]. Misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'', (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> Every year, many groups take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. There are several different groups practicing misogi in Japan, perhaps one of the best known is the Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at the [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan, where people perform misogi with cold water before sunrise. Another well known group is under the [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]]. Misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'', (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> Every year, many groups take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan.
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] (loin cloth) and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the ''[[kami]]'' around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "''harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!''" This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life And Thought Of A Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises]. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your ''[[Dantian|one-point]]'', or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.

This may be undertaken through exhaustive activities such as extended periods without sleep, breath training,<ref>[http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-PHIL/ew88808.htm Mind and Morality in Nineteenth-Century Japanese Religions: Misogi-Kyo and Maruyama-Kyo] By Janine Anderson Sawada Philosophy East & West V. 48 No. 1 (January 1998) pp. 108-141 Copyright 1998 by [[University of Hawaii]] Press</ref> standing under [[waterfall]]s, or other methods. Water-misogi may be likened to [[dousing]] practices.

Another well known group is under the [[Tsubaki Great Shrine]] in [[Mie prefecture]]. Misogi is performed under an outdoor waterfall mornings and evenings. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> Every year, many groups take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan.
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] (loin cloth) and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the ''[[kami]]'' around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "''harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!''" This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life And Thought Of A Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises]. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your ''[[Dantian|one-point]]'', or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection.

revision.comment: 1) This is a misunderstanding in reading the source which states that "misogi" and non-water-related activies are different and is mainly talking about the practices of two Shinto sects and not Misogi in particular. 2) Moved aikido stuff to the end.
revision.text:
[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body.
Misogi is related to another Shinto ritual purification called ''[[Harae]]'' – thus both being collectively refered to as {{Nihongo|Misogiharae|禊祓}}.
In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> Every year, many groups take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan. In the United states misogi is performed at the [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|accessdate=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] (loin cloth) and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the ''[[kami]]'' around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase "''harae-tamae-Kiyome-tamae-ro-kon-sho-jo!''" This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life And Thought Of A Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises]. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your ''[[Dantian|one-point]]'', or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.

Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan. In the United states misogi is performed at the [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|accessdate=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] (loin cloth) and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the ''[[kami]]'' around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harae tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓え給え清め給え六根清浄}} This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life And Thought Of A Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises]. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your ''[[Dantian|one-point]]'', or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5th ed. : Prentice Hall.
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Ritual purification]]

revision.text:
[[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''Misogi'''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual called ''[[Harae]]'' – thus both being collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|Misogiharae|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483|title=Misogi|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|author=Nishioka Kazuhiko|date=March 31, 2007|accessdate=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> Every year, many groups take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan. In the United States misogi is performed at the [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|accessdate=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white kimono and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] (loin cloth) and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the ''[[god]]'' around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''" to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}} This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life And Thought Of A Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises]. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[Aikido]] to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop your ''[[Dantian|one-point]]'', or centre. The founder of Aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]] regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[Temizuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. Living Religions. 5th ed. : Prentice Hall.
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Ritual purification]]

The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}} This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises]. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.

The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. revision.comment: Adding [[Wikipedia:Short description|short description]]: "Shinto practice of ritual purification by washing the entire body" ([[User:Galobtter/Shortdesc helper|Shortdesc helper]])
revision.text:
{{short description|Shinto practice of ritual purification by washing the entire body}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[Image:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual called ''[[Harae]]'' – thus both being collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|Misogiharae|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |work=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |accessdate=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> Every year, many groups take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for Misogi in Japan. In the United States misogi is performed at the [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|accessdate=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[kimono]] ([[robe]]) and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] ([[loin cloth]]) and head band. They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the [[spirit]]. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * [[Baptism]] * ''[[Ghusl]]'' * ''[[Mikveh]]'' * ''[[Temizuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall.

This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.

They then begin ''furitama'' (降り魂) or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] (''tori-fune'' 鳥船 or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.

{{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual called ''[[Harae]]'' – thus both being collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> In the United States misogi is performed at the [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[kimono]] ([[robe]]) and a headband and men put on a [[fundoshi]] ([[loincloth]]) and head band. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises are done so participants raise their [[metabolism]] and some groups accompany this with deep breathing. They may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine and then cuts the air while shouting the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[Temizuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Hydrotherapy#Recent techniques|Hydrotherapy]] ** [[Cryotherapy]] * [[Contrast bath therapy]] * [[Baptism]] * ''[[Ghusl]]'' * ''[[Mikveh]]''

The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[Chozuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Hydrotherapy#Recent techniques|Hydrotherapy]] ** [[Cryotherapy]] * [[Contrast bath therapy]] * [[Baptism]] * ''[[Ghusl]]'' * ''[[Mikveh]]''

{{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''[[harae]]''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In [[Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>[http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm Kiyomizu-dera]</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014}}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[kimono]] and men put on a [[fundoshi]] ([[loincloth]]), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises make participants raise their [[metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[Temizuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Hydrotherapy#Recent techniques|Hydrotherapy]] ** [[Cryotherapy]] * [[Contrast bath therapy]] * [[Baptism]] * ''[[Ghusl]]'' * ''[[Mikveh]]''

==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises make participants raise their [[metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.

Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''[[harae]]''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them.
The above exercises make participants raise their [[metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
