[[en:Misogi]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:ميسوجي]] [[az:Misoqi]] {{Commons category|Misogi}} [[cs:Misogi]] [[da:Misogi]] [[de:Misogi]] [[el:Μισόγκι]] [[es:Misogi]] [[fa:میسوگی]] [[fr:Misogi]] [[id:Misogi]] [[it:Misogi]] [[ja:禊]] [[ko:미소기]] [[te:మిసోగి]] [[tr:Misogi]] [[vi:Misogi]] [[zh:禊 (日本)]]
[[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q1936265}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1936265]] Wikidata name: misogi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[:en:Pilgrimage|Pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[:en:Kimono|Kimono]] and men put on a [[:en:Fundoshi|Fundoshi]] ([[:en:Loincloth|Loincloth]]), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[:en:Calisthenics|Calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their [[:en:Metabolism|Metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[:en:Dantian|Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[:en:Morihei Ueshiba|Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1936265]] Wikidata name: misogi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[:en:Pilgrimage|Pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[:en:Kimono|Kimono]] and men put on a [[:en:Fundoshi|Fundoshi]] ([[:en:Loincloth|Loincloth]]), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[:en:Calisthenics|Calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their [[:en:Metabolism|Metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[:en:Dantian|Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[:en:Morihei Ueshiba|Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1936265]] Wikidata name: misogi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[:en:Pilgrimage|Pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[:en:Kimono|Kimono]] and men put on a [[:en:Fundoshi|Fundoshi]] ([[:en:Loincloth|Loincloth]]), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[:en:Calisthenics|Calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their [[:en:Metabolism|Metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[:en:Dantian|Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[:en:Morihei Ueshiba|Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1936265]] Wikidata name: misogi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take [[:en:Pilgrimage|Pilgrimage]]s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America]] at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white [[:en:Kimono|Kimono]] and men put on a [[:en:Fundoshi|Fundoshi]] ([[:en:Loincloth|Loincloth]]), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or [[:en:Calisthenics|Calisthenics]] ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their [[:en:Metabolism|Metabolism]], and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]], to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''[[:en:Dantian|Dantian]]'', or centre. The founder of aikido, [[:en:Morihei Ueshiba|Morihei Ueshiba]], regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''[[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]]'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1936265]] Wikidata name: misogi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}}]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae}}''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|WD=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage}}s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|WD=Q840967|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America}} at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|WD=Q483444|lt=Kimono}} and men put on a {{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|WD=Q976963|lt=Fundoshi}} ({{ill|Loincloth|en|Loincloth|WD=Q1130359|lt=Loincloth}}), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or {{ill|Calisthenics|en|Calisthenics|WD=Q2471790|lt=Calisthenics}} ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their {{ill|Metabolism|en|Metabolism|WD=Q1057|lt=Metabolism}}, and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|WD=Q1165048|lt=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|WD=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

[[en:Misogi]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:ميسوجي]] [[az:Misoqi]] {{Commons category|Misogi}} [[cs:Misogi]] [[da:Misogi]] [[de:Misogi]] [[el:Μισόγκι]] [[es:Misogi]] [[fa:میسوگی]] [[fr:Misogi]] [[id:Misogi]] [[it:Misogi]] [[ja:禊]] [[ko:미소기]] [[te:మిసోగి]] [[tr:Misogi]] [[vi:Misogi]] [[zh:禊 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q1936265}} [[Category:Bathing in Japan]] [[Category:Exorcism in Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ritual purification]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Water in Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]]
{{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}}]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae}}''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|WD=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage}}s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|WD=Q840967|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America}} at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|WD=Q483444|lt=Kimono}} and men put on a {{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|WD=Q976963|lt=Fundoshi}} ({{ill|Loincloth|en|Loincloth|WD=Q1130359|lt=Loincloth}}), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or {{ill|Calisthenics|en|Calisthenics|WD=Q2471790|lt=Calisthenics}} ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their {{ill|Metabolism|en|Metabolism|WD=Q1057|lt=Metabolism}}, and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|WD=Q1165048|lt=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|WD=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[sn:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]]
{{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}}]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|WD=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage|1=Pilgrimage}}s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|WD=Q840967|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America}} at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|WD=Q483444|lt=Kimono|1=Kimono}} and men put on a {{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|WD=Q976963|lt=Fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}} ({{ill|Loincloth|en|Loincloth|WD=Q1130359|lt=Loincloth|1=Loincloth}}), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or {{ill|Calisthenics|en|Calisthenics|WD=Q2471790|lt=Calisthenics|1=Calisthenics}} ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their {{ill|Metabolism|en|Metabolism|WD=Q1057|lt=Metabolism|1=Metabolism}}, and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|WD=Q1165048|lt=Dantian|1=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|WD=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba|1=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|WD=Q1165048|lt=Dantian|1=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|WD=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba|1=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[co:commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:ميسوجي]][[az:Misoqi]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Misogi]][[cs:Misogi]][[da:Misogi]][[de:Misogi]][[el:Μισόγκι]][[en:Misogi]][[es:Misogi]][[fa:میسوگی]][[fr:Misogi]][[id:Misogi]][[it:Misogi]][[ja:禊]][[ko:미소기]][[te:మిసోగి]][[tr:Misogi]][[vi:Misogi]][[zh:禊 (日本)]]
{{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}}]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|WD=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage|1=Pilgrimage}}s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|WD=Q840967|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America}} at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|WD=Q483444|lt=Kimono|1=Kimono}} and men put on a {{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|WD=Q976963|lt=Fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}} ({{ill|Loincloth|en|Loincloth|WD=Q1130359|lt=Loincloth|1=Loincloth}}), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or {{ill|Calisthenics|en|Calisthenics|WD=Q2471790|lt=Calisthenics|1=Calisthenics}} ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their {{ill|Metabolism|en|Metabolism|WD=Q1057|lt=Metabolism|1=Metabolism}}, and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|WD=Q1165048|lt=Dantian|1=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|WD=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba|1=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or {{ill|Calisthenics|en|Calisthenics|qid=Q2471790|lt=Calisthenics|1=Calisthenics}} ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their {{ill|Metabolism|en|Metabolism|qid=Q1057|lt=Metabolism|1=Metabolism}}, and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|qid=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|qid=Q1165048|lt=Dantian|1=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|qid=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba|1=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * Fisher, Mary Pat. ''Living Religions'', 5th ed. Prentice Hall. * [http://www.shugendo-austria.org/praktiken/takigyo-wasserrituale/ Examples of different Misogi & Takigyo practices from the Yamabushi Christian Grübl (Austria)]

{{short description|Shinto practice}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Misogi Tsubaki-Jinja.jpg|thumb|Night misogi under a waterfall at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|qid=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}}]] {{Shintoism}} {{Nihongo|'''''Misogi'''''|禊}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] practice of [[ritual purification]] by washing the entire body. Misogi is related to another Shinto purification ritual, ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|qid=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}''. Thus, both are collectively referred to as {{Nihongo|''misogiharae''|禊祓}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 |title=Misogi |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |author=Nishioka Kazuhiko |date=March 31, 2007 |access-date=February 11, 2014 |archive-date=February 22, 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222100739/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1483 }}</ref>
==Background== Every year, many people take {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|qid=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage|1=Pilgrimage}}s to sacred waterfalls, lakes and rivers, either alone or in small groups, to perform misogi. Mount Ontake, the Kii mountain range and Mount Yoshino are but a few examples of ancient and well known areas for misogi in Japan. In {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, people douse themselves under [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu]] Temple's ''Otowa no taki'' (Sound-of-Wings) waterfall, although the majority of visitors drink from the waters rather than plunging into them.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm|title=Kiyomizu-dera|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 28, 2004|quote=bottom of page|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040828053810/http://farstrider.net/Japan/Kyoto/Kiyomizudera.htm }}</ref> In the United States, misogi was performed at the {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|qid=Q840967|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America}} at the Konryu Myojin no Taki waterfall each morning in the years prior to its closure in 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html|title=Misogi Shuho|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America|access-date=February 11, 2014|archive-date=February 21, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140221174222/http://tsubakishrine.com/misogishuho/index.html }}</ref>
Before encountering misogi, members generally undergo some sort of preliminary purification. Such things as prayers, fasting, or some sort of physical activity is common. Generally, women put on a special white {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|qid=Q483444|lt=Kimono|1=Kimono}} and men put on a {{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|qid=Q976963|lt=Fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}} ({{ill|Loincloth|en|Loincloth|qid=Q1130359|lt=Loincloth|1=Loincloth}}), with both genders also wearing headbands. They then begin {{Nihongo|''furitama''|降り魂}} or "spirit shaking" by clenching their hands in front of the stomach and shaking them up and down, vibrating the upper torso. The purpose of this is to become aware of/unified with the spirit's presence within. Following this is a "warm-up" or {{ill|Calisthenics|en|Calisthenics|qid=Q2471790|lt=Calisthenics|1=Calisthenics}} ({{Nihongo|''tori-fune''|鳥船}} or "bird boat" rowing). These two aforementioned practices are sometimes accompanied by special prayers or incantations. After, the leader begins to speak out invocations/prayers that are said to activate the spirit. The followers generally speak along with them, thus affirming the potential for realizing one's own spirit, and thus unifying them with the [[kami]] around them. The above exercises make participants raise their {{ill|Metabolism|en|Metabolism|qid=Q1057|lt=Metabolism|1=Metabolism}}, and some groups accompany that with deep breathing. The exercises may be sprinkled with purifying salt and may be given [[sake]] to spit into the waterfall in three mouthfuls. Sometimes the participants are given salt to throw into the waterfall as they enter. In some groups, the leader counts to nine, cuts the air, and shouts the word "''yei!''{{-"}} to dispel this impurity. The participants then enter the waterfall while continuously chanting the phrase {{Nihongo|''harai tamae kiyome tamae rokkon shōjō''|祓い給え清め給え六根清浄}}. This phrase asks the ''kami'' to wash away the impurity from the six elements that make up the human being, the five senses and the mind. The practice of this varies from group to group, each having their own traditions or methods.<ref>[http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html Kami no Michi: The Life and Thought of a Shinto Priest. Appendix C: Misogi and Spiritual Exercises] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120219003348/http://www.csuchico.edu/~gwilliams/tsa/Appendix_c.html |date=2012-02-19 }}. Guji Yukitaka Yamamoto. California State University. Retrieved 2011-11-14.</ref>
Misogi is also used in some forms of martial arts, especially {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|qid=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}, to prepare the mind for training and to learn how to develop one's ''{{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|qid=Q1165048|lt=Dantian|1=Dantian}}'', or centre. The founder of aikido, {{ill|Morihei Ueshiba|en|Morihei Ueshiba|qid=Q182130|lt=Morihei Ueshiba|1=Morihei Ueshiba}}, regularly used this form of meditation to complement his training and search for perfection. The Sen Shin tei Misogi Well at [[Ki Society]] Headquarters in Japan is a well-known place for people performing misogi with cold water before sunrise.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}'', a pavilion for ritual purification at the entrance to Shinto shrines * [[Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi|Purification of Izanagi]]
